entities.name: Los Alamos National Laboratory
entities.notes: Los Alamos National Laboratory (LANL) is one of 17 U.S. Department of Energy national laboratories, overseen specifically by the National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA). Founded during the Manhattan Project, it is one of the world's foremost research institutions with an expected budget approaching $5 billion as of FY2025, more than double its 2019 level. LANL actively funds and conducts advanced AI and computing research: in 2024 it awarded the University of Michigan a five-year, $15 million grant to develop AI, sophisticated modeling, bioinformatics, genomics, and quantum sciences. In 2022, the DOE selected LANL to lead a $9.25 million SciDAC collaborative project on nuclear energy materials modeling. LANL plays a principal role in the DOE's Quantum Science Center, which received renewed funding of $125 million over five years for quantum-accelerated high-performance computing. The lab applies AI and advanced computing to challenges including turbulent plasma modeling for fusion energy, nuclear warhead pit production, and legacy waste cleanup. Its dual mission—national security (nuclear weapons modernization) and open science—makes it a uniquely influential node in the U.S. AI and scientific computing ecosystem.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: As a federal national laboratory operating under DOE/NNSA, LANL does not articulate independent regulatory stances on AI policy. Its research posture—funding AI development across universities and leading large-scale computing programs—suggests an orientation toward accelerating AI capabilities in the national interest, but formal policy positions are set by DOE and NNSA leadership rather than the laboratory itself.

entities.name: Mack Institute for Innovation Management (Wharton)
entities.notes: The Mack Institute for Innovation Management is a research and education center at the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania, established in 2001 through a gift from William L. Mack (W'61) and Phyllis Mack, and elevated to institute status in 2013. It serves as a hub connecting academic research, business practice, and student experiential learning around innovation management. The institute has funded over 800 research proposals totaling more than $5.5 million since its founding, supporting Wharton faculty and PhD students including work on AI and emerging technologies. It shapes the AI ecosystem by funding faculty research on topics such as generative AI in education, algorithmic management, and AI's impact on the gig economy, including the $11,000 2022 grant to Professor Prasanna (Sonny) Tambe. The institute runs the Collaborative Innovation Program (CIP), which places graduate students in companies to solve innovation challenges, and hosts the annual Wharton Technology and Innovation Conference co-sponsored with Wharton's Management Department. Pre-doctoral fellows at the institute conduct research on generative AI's effects on online communities and organizations, indicating a meaningful footprint in AI-adjacent academic research.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Mack Institute is an academic research center focused on innovation management; its publications and programs do not articulate an explicit public stance on AI regulation. Its activities—funding research and facilitating industry-academic exchange—suggest a pragmatic, research-driven orientation, but no direct policy positions are evident from available sources.

entities.name: Matthew J. Salganik
entities.notes: Matthew J. Salganik is the Alexander Stewart 1886 Professor of Sociology at Princeton University, widely recognized as a leading figure in computational social science and social network research. He is the author of 'Bit by Bit: Social Research in the Digital Age,' a foundational text in computational social science, and co-founder (with Chris Bail) of the Summer Institute in Computational Social Science (SICSS), which trains graduate students and faculty in computational methods. His research has been funded by NSF, NIH, UNAIDS, Russell Sage Foundation, Alfred P. Sloan Foundation, Facebook, and Google, with grants ranging from methodology improvements (network scale-up, respondent-driven sampling) to wiki surveys and algorithmic fairness. He is affiliated with Princeton's AI Lab and the Center for Information Technology Policy (CITP), connecting his sociological research directly to AI and technology policy concerns. He has served on the Board of Directors of Mathematica Policy Research since July 2018, and his work has been featured in Science, PNAS, Nature, the New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Economist, and New Yorker. His research areas include social networks, computational social science, algorithmic risk assessments, and methods for studying hard-to-reach populations, all of which have direct relevance to AI fairness, bias, and societal impact debates.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance on AI policy is documented in the sources. entities.name: MIT Quest for Intelligence (MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence)
entities.notes: The MIT Quest for Intelligence (now MIT Siegel Family Quest for Intelligence) is a campus-wide initiative launched on February 1, 2018, at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, dedicated to understanding the foundations of human and machine intelligence and developing AI technologies that can benefit society. The initiative has two linked entities: 'The Core,' focused on the science and engineering of intelligence, and 'The Bridge,' dedicated to applying MIT discoveries in AI across all disciplines. Antonio Torralba was named inaugural director in June 2018; Jim DiCarlo later became director when the Quest joined the MIT Stephen A. Schwarzman College of Computing in January 2020. The initiative received early philanthropic support from Eric Schmidt and his wife Wendy, as well as from Google, and was more recently re-endowed through a major gift from the Siegel Family Endowment. Its roots trace to the NSF-funded Center for Brains, Minds and Machines (CBMM), established in 2013. The Quest brings together neuroscience, cognitive science, and computer science researchers and has significant industry and philanthropic backing, positioning MIT as a major hub for AI research and policy influence.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Quest's launch materials emphasize 'thoughtful' development of AI and the importance of human values in AI systems, as expressed by advisors at the 2018 kickoff event. The initiative focuses on research and tools to ensure AI positively influences society, suggesting a balanced, responsible-development stance rather than either accelerationist or restrictive positions.

entities.name: LASR Labs (London AI Safety Research Labs)
entities.notes: LASR Labs (London AI Safety Research Labs) is a 13-week, full-time, in-person technical AI safety research training programme based in London, founded and operated under the umbrella of Arcadia Impact (Charity Number: 1212323), with additional grant support from Coefficient Giving. The programme is designed to transition engineers and scientists into full-time AI safety research careers by having participants work in teams of three to four, supervised by experienced researchers from institutions such as Google DeepMind, UK AISI, and top UK universities, to produce academic papers and blog posts on key AI safety problems. The programme offers participants an £11,000 stipend plus food, office space, and travel for the 13-week duration, and operates from the London Initiative for Safe AI (LISA) office alongside organizations like Apollo Research, Leap Labs, and Bluedot Impact. Research topics have included sparse autoencoders and feature absorption, steganographic collusion in LLMs, deception and reward hacking, interpretability probes, multi-agent collusion, AI control, and scalable oversight. Alumni from previous cohorts (2023–2025) have gone on to roles at the UK AI Security Institute, OpenAI's Preparedness/dangerous capabilities evals team, Apollo Research, Open Philanthropy, and Leap Labs. Publication success has been strong: four out of five 2023 papers were accepted to NeurIPS workshops or ICLR, and all five Summer 2024 papers were accepted to NeurIPS workshops. The programme is led by Programme Director Erin Robertson, who also serves as Co-Director of Arcadia Impact, with advisory support from UK AISI researcher Charlie Griffin.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: LASR Labs does not publish explicit regulatory positions, but its entire mission is premised on the urgency of reducing risks from advanced AI before loss of control occurs. The programme focuses on 'action-relevant questions tackling concrete threat models' and explicitly frames its work around preventing catastrophic outcomes, suggesting alignment with a precautionary worldview that AI development requires serious safety infrastructure now.

entities.name: Safeguarded AI Programme
entities.notes: The Safeguarded AI Programme is a £59 million (~$60M USD) R&D initiative housed within the UK's Advanced Research and Invention Agency (ARIA), launched in 2024 and led by Programme Director David 'davidad' Dalrymple. The programme sits within ARIA's 'Mathematics for Safe AI' opportunity space and aims to develop quantitative safety guarantees for AI systems—analogous to rigorous safety standards in nuclear power and passenger aviation. Its core thesis involves constructing a 'gatekeeper' AI that uses scientific world models and mathematical proofs to verify that other AI agents operate only within agreed-upon safety guardrails in a given application (e.g., balancing electricity grids, optimising clinical trials). The programme is divided into three Technical Areas: TA1 (Scaffolding, including theory, backend, HCI, and sociotechnical integration), TA2 (Machine Learning, awarded £18M to a single non-profit entity to develop verifiable ML capabilities), and TA3 (Applications, initially funded at £5.4M across ~10 teams to prototype domain-specific deployments). Notably, ARIA redirected its efforts in late 2024 away from TA2 as originally planned, doubling down on TA1 and pivoting TA3 focus toward cybersecurity. The programme has issued multiple solicitations including a £14.2M call for TA1.2 and TA1.3 software developers (deadline April 2025) and a £1M Phase 1 for TA2 organizations. The programme represents one of the most substantial government-funded efforts globally focused specifically on formal verification and guaranteed-safe AI methods.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The programme does not advocate for restrictive AI regulation per se, but aims to develop the technical safety standards and quantitative guarantees that would underpin responsible AI governance—analogous to engineering safety standards in aviation and nuclear power. This implies support for a structured, standards-based regulatory framework rather than either laissez-faire or prescriptive mandates.

entities.name: The Alignment Project
entities.notes: The Alignment Project is a global AI alignment research funding programme launched in July 2025 by the UK AI Security Institute (AISI), initially with over £15 million in funding. It is backed by an international cross-sector coalition of government, industry, and philanthropic funders including the UK AI Security Institute, Canadian AI Safety Institute (CAISI), CIFAR, Australian AI Safety Institute, OpenAI, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services, Schmidt Sciences, Anthropic, AI Safety Tactical Opportunities Fund, Halcyon Futures, Safe AI Fund, Sympatico Ventures, Renaissance Philanthropy, UK Research and Innovation, and ARIA. In February 2026, OpenAI ($7.5M / £5.6M) and Microsoft joined as new funders, bringing total available funding to over £27 million (approximately $36.3 million). In its first round, grants ranging from £50,000 to £1,000,000 were awarded to over 60 projects across eight countries. The project offers three modes of support: grant funding, cloud compute resources (via AWS and others), and venture capital investment for alignment-focused startups. It is guided by an expert advisory board including Yoshua Bengio, Zico Kolter, Shafi Goldwasser, Buck Shlegeris, Andrea Lincoln, Marcelo Mattar, and Sydney Levine. A second funding round is expected to open in summer 2026.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Alignment Project reflects a government-led but internationally collaborative stance: it does not advocate for broad AI prohibitions but actively funds technical safety research to ensure AI systems remain under human control and operate as intended. The UK government framing emphasizes that alignment research is a shared international responsibility and a precondition for safe deployment of advanced AI, suggesting a targeted, safety-first regulatory posture rather than a restrictive or laissez-faire one.

entities.name: Virtual AI Safety Unconference (VAISU)
entities.notes: The Virtual AI Safety Unconference (VAISU) is a free-access, participant-driven online event series for AI safety researchers, both established and emerging, running at least since 2023. The 2023 edition (July 31–August 6, 2023) had approximately 280 registered participants, 41 sessions, 14 volunteers, and 2 staff. The 2024 edition (May 23–26, 2024) was co-organized with AI Safety Camp and featured 400 registered participants, 40 community-led sessions, and a $2,000 USD-equivalent prize for impactful sessions. VAISU operates on a 'pay what you want' and donation/grant model, with a $10,000 USD funding goal for the 2024 edition; funding came in part from LTFF (Long-Term Future Fund) and small individual donors. The event covers topics including alignment, corrigibility, interpretability, AI governance, and strategy, and its unconference format allows participants to shape the agenda. It is organized under the Horizon Omega umbrella, which is planning a hybrid AI Safety Unconference (AISU) edition for 2026. The event serves as a community connector and knowledge-sharing platform within the AI safety ecosystem.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: VAISU's scope includes AI governance and 'slowing down AI and strategy' as explicit topics, suggesting some openness to precautionary or restrictive approaches, but as an unconference the event does not take a single institutional stance on regulation.

entities.name: Parallel (beparallel.com)
entities.notes: Parallel is a Paris-based startup founded in 2025 by Paul Lafforgue (CEO, École Polytechnique graduate, former Meta and McKinsey) and Christopher Rydahl (CTO, founder of Hublo, Europe's largest healthcare staffing platform). The company builds AI agents that automate administrative workflows in hospitals — including medical coding, billing, and admissions — by operating as an AI layer on top of existing legacy systems rather than requiring deep integration, enabling deployment in as little as one week. Within less than a year of its $3.5M seed round, Parallel had deployed agents across several dozen public and private hospitals in Europe. On March 19, 2026, the company closed a $20M Series A led by Index Ventures, with participation from Frst, Y Combinator, Hexa, and angels including Arthur Mensch (Mistral AI CEO), Felix Blossier, Quentin de Metz (Pennylane founders), and Vincent Bouvier (Vidal). The company's AI uses Large Language Models and Computer Use Agents to process medical data, improve coding accuracy, and capture appropriate hospital reimbursement revenue. Parallel is a YC alum and plans to expand internationally and broaden its agent portfolio across hospital operations.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit public statements on AI regulation found in available sources. The company operates in a highly regulated healthcare environment and emphasizes accuracy and compliance in medical coding and billing, suggesting awareness of regulatory constraints, but no formal policy position has been articulated.

entities.name: Recraft
entities.notes: Recraft is an AI-native generative design platform founded in 2022 and headquartered in London, UK. The company was founded by Anna Dorogush Veronika and focuses on enabling creative teams to generate and edit digital illustrations, vector art, icons, and 3D graphics with consistent brand styling. Recraft's proprietary model, Recraft V3 (released late October 2024), topped the Artificial Analysis leaderboard for text-to-image generation models, outperforming competitors including OpenAI's DALL-E and Midjourney. The company raised a $12M Series A in January 2024 led by Khosla Ventures, followed by a $30M Series B in May 2025 led by Accel with participation from Madrona Ventures, Khosla Ventures, Nat Friedman, Elad Gil, and RTP Global, bringing total funding to approximately $42M. As of mid-2025, Recraft has surpassed 4 million users and exceeded $5 million in annual recurring revenue (ARR). The platform shapes the AI ecosystem by building and deploying frontier image-generation models and providing professional designers with AI-powered tools that directly compete with major generative AI image platforms.

entities.name: Recursion Pharmaceuticals
entities.notes: Recursion Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ: RXRX) is a clinical-stage TechBio company founded in 2013 by Chris Gibson, Blake Borgeson, and Dean Li at the University of Utah, focused on using AI and automation to industrialize drug discovery and development. The company operates the Recursion OS, a closed-loop platform combining high-throughput wet-lab biology with machine learning to generate one of the world's largest proprietary biological and chemical datasets (50+ petabytes as of 2025). Recursion completed its IPO on NASDAQ in 2021 and has raised funding across multiple rounds, with Lux Capital leading its Series A in 2017 and a $60M Series B shortly after. The company has built BioHive-2, ranked as the 76th most powerful supercomputer globally (Top500, 2025), developed in partnership with NVIDIA, and has expanded partnerships with Google Cloud and Tempus. In 2023-2024, Recursion acquired Cyclica and Valence to bolster generative AI and digital chemistry capabilities, and announced a combination with Exscientia to advance AI-driven drug industrialization. The company is a prominent example of AI applied to pharmaceutical R&D at scale, with a pipeline of 50+ programs across oncology and rare diseases, and serves as a key builder and deployer of AI in biotech.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Recursion's public messaging emphasizes speed, disruption of the status quo, and removing human bias through AI-driven empiricism. The company frames itself as challenging conservative norms in pharma and advocates for industrializing drug discovery through automation and AI with minimal friction. entities.name: Toyota Motor Corporation
entities.notes: Toyota Motor Corporation is one of the world's largest automotive manufacturers and a major funder of AI and autonomous vehicle research. In September 2015, Toyota announced a $50 million, five-year investment to establish joint research centers at MIT (Toyota-CSAIL Joint Research Center, $25 million) and Stanford University to advance autonomous vehicle technologies, robotics, and AI. The MIT center was led by CSAIL Director Daniela Rus and focused on decision-making algorithms, computer vision, perception, planning, and control for autonomous systems. Dr. Gill Pratt, formerly of DARPA, was hired by Toyota to direct the overall collaborative research program. In a second phase, Toyota's Research Institute (TRI) committed over $75 million to expand the academic collaboration to 13 additional universities including Carnegie Mellon, Columbia, UPenn, Princeton, UC-Berkeley, and UCLA, generating 69 patent applications and nearly 650 research papers in its first five-year phase alone. Toyota's AI investments reflect a broader corporate strategy to eliminate traffic fatalities and develop intelligent mobility solutions, positioning it as one of the most active automotive-sector funders of foundational AI research globally.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No explicit regulatory stance is stated in the sources. Their framing centers on voluntary safety goals ('eliminating traffic casualties') rather than advocating for government mandates.

entities.name: Public Interest Tech Lab (Harvard Kennedy School)
entities.notes: The Public Interest Tech Lab (PITL) is a research initiative launched in June 2021 at Harvard Kennedy School's Shorenstein Center on Media, Politics and Public Policy, founded and directed by Dr. Latanya Sweeney, the Daniel Paul Professor of the Practice of Government and Technology at Harvard. It was established with a $3 million grant from the Ford Foundation. The lab's mission is to ensure technology products are socially responsible, focusing on ways technology can serve—rather than harm—people and society, with particular emphasis on data privacy, algorithmic fairness, and equity. It operates across three pillars: creating and providing public-interest technologies, enabling research and development, and sharing knowledge across institutions and disciplines via networks like the Public Interest Technology University Network. The lab grew out of Sweeney's earlier Data Privacy Lab, founded at Carnegie Mellon in 2001 and brought to Harvard in 2011, reflecting decades of work on technology-society clashes. Sweeney, a former Chief Technology Officer at the U.S. Federal Trade Commission and the first Black woman to earn a PhD in Computer Science from MIT, brings a strong regulatory and policy perspective to the lab's work. Research areas include voting, vaccine access, algorithmic discrimination, and other societal challenges with technological implications, shaping both academic discourse and policy on AI and data systems.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The lab's core mission is to ensure technology products are 'socially responsible,' framing harms from algorithms and data systems as unresolved societal clashes requiring active intervention. Sweeney's prior role as FTC CTO and her research on algorithmic discrimination and data privacy signal support for robust oversight and accountability requirements, not voluntary self-governance.

entities.name: The Ethics & Governance of AI Initiative
entities.notes: The Ethics and Governance of AI Initiative (also written as Ethics + Governance of Artificial Intelligence Initiative) was launched in 2017 as a joint project of the MIT Media Lab and the Harvard Berkman-Klein Center for Internet and Society. It functions as both a hybrid research effort and a philanthropic fund, with a mission to ensure that AI and machine learning technologies are developed and deployed in ways that uphold fairness, human autonomy, and justice. The Initiative incubates research, prototyping, and advocacy activities across its two anchor institutions and the broader civil society ecosystem. Its work is focused on three primary domains: AI and Justice (including criminal justice risk assessment), information quality, and AI governance structures. In 2018, the Initiative partnered with and funded the Human Rights Data Analysis Group (HRDAG) to support technical work evaluating automated risk assessment tools in the criminal justice system and to integrate causal modeling approaches into existing fairness frameworks. The Initiative is primarily funded through philanthropic sources, consistent with its positioning as a civil society-anchored research and grantmaking body. It has served as a key connector and funder bridging academic AI research with civil society and human rights organizations.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Initiative's stated mission focuses on ensuring AI is developed in ways that 'vindicate social values of fairness, human autonomy, and justice,' and it explicitly focuses on legal and institutional structures for governing AI in public administration. Its work on criminal justice risk assessment and information quality reflects a targeted, rights-based regulatory orientation rather than either a laissez-faire or a precautionary approach.

entities.name: Nick D'Aloisio
entities.notes: Nick D'Aloisio (born 1 November 1995) is a British computer programmer and internet entrepreneur best known for founding Summly, a mobile app that used NLP algorithms to automatically summarize news articles. He became the world's youngest venture capital-backed entrepreneur at age 16 when he received $300,000 from Li Ka-Shing's Horizon Ventures in 2011, and later raised $1.23M from celebrity investors including Yoko Ono, Ashton Kutcher, and Rupert Murdoch. Yahoo acquired Summly for approximately $30 million in March 2013, when D'Aloisio was 17, making him one of the youngest self-made millionaires in history. He served as a product manager at Yahoo leading the News Digest app (which won an Apple Design Award in 2014) before leaving in late 2015 to study Philosophy and Computer Science at Oxford. In late 2015 he co-founded Sphere Knowledge, a knowledge-sharing startup that raised $30M from Index Ventures and Mike Moritz, which was acquired by Twitter in October 2021. He completed a BPhil in Philosophy at Oxford in July 2021 and is now pursuing a DPhil, with seven papers accepted in peer-reviewed journals. His work in natural language processing and AI-powered summarization places him at the intersection of AI research and entrepreneurship, and his connection to DeepMind's investor network (via Founders Fund/Peter Thiel ecosystem) further ties him to the broader AI funding landscape.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: No public statements or discernible positions on AI regulation found in available sources. His career has focused on building and selling AI-powered products rather than policy advocacy.

entities.name: Ronald D. Sugar
entities.notes: Ronald D. entities.name: Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada
entities.notes: The Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council (SSHRC) is a Canadian federal research funding agency created in 1977 under the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council Act. Its mandate is to promote and support postsecondary-based research and research training in the social sciences and humanities, and to advise the Minister on related matters. SSHRC funds research through grants, fellowships, and scholarships across three program streams: Research Training and Talent Development, Insight Research, and Research Partnerships. In the 2024–2025 fiscal year, SSHRC received 14,100 funding applications and awarded more than 5,000 new grants and fellowships totaling significant sums; for example, Insight Grants alone distributed over $86 million across 503 awards. SSHRC shapes the AI ecosystem by funding social science and humanities researchers who study AI's economic, social, and policy implications—most notably it has provided ongoing funding to prominent AI economists Avi Goldfarb and Ajay Agrawal at the University of Toronto, whose work on the economics of AI and machine learning has been highly influential in AI policy debates. SSHRC also administers several national research programs on behalf of all three federal research funding agencies (alongside CIHR and NSERC), including the Canada Research Chairs and New Frontiers in Research Fund, which can support interdisciplinary AI-related research. It hosts the Canada Research Coordinating Committee secretariat and the Tri-agency Institutional Programs Secretariat.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: As a federal funding agency, SSHRC does not publicly articulate a direct regulatory stance on AI. Its mandate focuses on funding research and advising the Minister on social sciences and humanities matters; its implicit stance supports generating the knowledge base—including social, ethical, and economic analyses of AI—needed to inform balanced policy, suggesting moderate, evidence-based governance rather than a specific regulatory posture.

Public First is a super PAC (FEC ID C00930503) that is part of the broader Public First network founded by former Democratic Congressman Brad Carson and former Republican Congressman Chris Stewart, formed in 2025 to advocate for responsible AI regulation. As of March 2026, the super PAC has raised approximately $1.5 million, with top donors including Michael Cohen ($500,000) and Public First Action ($1 million). The PAC has disbursed funds to aligned PACs including Jobs and Democracy PAC ($740,000) and Defending Our Values PAC ($300,000). The broader Public First Action 501(c)(4) received a $20 million donation from Anthropic in February 2026, with overall network fundraising goals of $50–$75 million for the 2026 midterms. The network directly opposes the pro-deregulation 'Leading the Future' super PAC funded by Andreessen Horowitz, Greg Brockman, and Joe Lonsdale.

[Note: This is Public First (research/polling firm). Public First Action (political advocacy, #1439) is a separate entity.]
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: Public First explicitly supports targeted AI regulation including export controls on advanced AI chips, transparency requirements for frontier AI labs, child online safety (KOSA), and mitigating AI-enabled bioweapons and cyberattacks. It opposes federal preemption of state AI laws without adequate federal safeguards, and supports minimum safety standards to prevent a race to the bottom among developers. Co-founder Brad Carson stated: 'We have $50 million and 85% of the public sentiment' in support of responsible AI governance.

She is also a Manifund regrantor for 2025 with a $100,000 budget, funding neglected work in AI security (SL5-relevant research, hardware security) and technical AI governance (flexHEGs, compute governance, export controls). She holds academic affiliations with Georgia Tech, TUM, CDTM, and UCL, and is a Brains Fellow.

entities.name: Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD)
entities.notes: The OECD is an international organisation comprising 38 member countries that works to build better policies for better lives, promoting economic and social well-being globally. It operates OECD.AI, a dedicated AI policy observatory and platform that tracks AI policy initiatives across member and non-member countries, making it a central node in global AI governance discourse. The OECD adopted the OECD Principles on AI in 2019, which became the basis for the G20 AI Principles and have influenced AI regulatory frameworks worldwide including the EU AI Act. Through its Development Co-operation Directorate (DCD), the OECD is actively working on digital public infrastructure (DPI) policy, including a new DAC work stream on digital transformation funded in part by the Gates Foundation ($2.4M) to establish a donor collaboration platform on DPI. The OECD Going Digital Integrated Policy Framework and Digital Government Policy Framework provide analytical tools helping governments design inclusive, trustworthy digital and AI policies. The OECD also publishes the Going Digital Toolkit and maintains the STIP Compass and other platforms to support evidence-based AI and digital policymaking. As a standard-setter and convener for 38 member governments plus partners, the OECD holds significant structural influence over how nations approach AI regulation, data governance, and digital infrastructure globally.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The OECD consistently advocates for balanced, evidence-based AI governance that promotes innovation while establishing trustworthy safeguards. Its 2019 AI Principles call for responsible stewardship, transparency, and accountability without prescribing heavy-handed mandates. The Going Digital Integrated Policy Framework emphasizes navigating trade-offs between public policy objectives, reflecting a moderate, targeted approach to digital and AI regulation.

entities.name: The MITRE Corporation
entities.notes: MITRE Corporation is a nonprofit, federally funded research and development organization (FFRDC) that works across national security, cybersecurity, and emerging technology domains. In December 2025, NIST announced a $20 million partnership with MITRE to establish two AI-focused research centers: the AI Economic Security Center for U.S. Manufacturing Productivity and the AI Economic Security Center to Secure U.S. Critical Infrastructure from Cyberthreats. MITRE has operated NIST's National Cybersecurity FFRDC (NCF) since 2014 and brings deep government-industry-academia collaboration capabilities to AI governance and applied research. The organization maintains several publicly available AI and cybersecurity tools including the ATT&CK, ATLAS, and CALDERA frameworks, as well as a state-of-the-art AI Lab and Federal AI Sandbox. The two new centers are designed to implement the White House's America's AI Action Plan and support NIST's Center for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI, formerly the U.S. AI Safety Institute). CEO Mark Peters emphasized MITRE's goal of producing 'transformational research to accelerate U.S. development and adoption of reliable, secure, and trustworthy AI.' MITRE's role as a trusted government partner positions it as a key institutional node in the U.S. federal AI policy and R&D ecosystem.

entities.name: Vanderbilt University Institute of National Security
entities.notes: The Vanderbilt University Institute of National Security is an academic research institute at Vanderbilt University, officially launched in fall 2024 under the founding directorship of retired General Paul M. Nakasone, former Director of the National Security Agency and Commander of U.S. Cyber Command. The Institute was conceived in 2023 as part of Discovery Vanderbilt and is housed in Vanderbilt's School of Engineering, with interdisciplinary involvement across engineering, AI, cybersecurity, law, policy, and other fields. Its mission is to develop future national security leaders and produce pragmatic, solutions-oriented research on pressing national security challenges. In February 2026, the Institute was awarded a $2.5 million, two-year grant (Oct 2025–Oct 2027) from the William & Flora Hewlett Foundation to support its Wicked Problems Lab and develop the Synthetic Information Filtering Testbed (SIFT), a first-of-its-kind platform designed to help researchers and policymakers understand and counter AI-driven information warfare and influence operations. SIFT integrates capabilities to identify propaganda networks, detect synthetic messages in organic content, and simulate adversarial AI influence campaigns, with a planned public release as an operational testbed. The Institute also conducts convenings such as the annual National Security Symposium and the Summit on Modern Conflict and Emerging Threats, and offers student immersion programs and speaker series. It shapes the AI ecosystem by directly researching AI-enabled threats, advising policymakers on regulatory responses to AI-powered influence operations, and bridging government, academia, and industry on national security AI challenges.

entities.name: Musk Foundation
entities.notes: The Musk Foundation is the personal philanthropic organization of Elon Musk, established in December 2001 with his brother Kimbal Musk and initially registered in Los Angeles before relocating to Austin, Texas. It funds renewable energy research and advocacy, human space exploration, pediatric research, science and engineering education, and the 'development of safe artificial intelligence to benefit humanity.' The foundation has been a notable funder of AI safety and policy organizations, including a $10 million grant to the Future of Life Institute in 2015 and early support for OpenAI (when it was a nonprofit), and it funds CSET (Center for Security and Emerging Technology) at Georgetown. The foundation disbursed a record $474 million in grants in 2024, its largest year of giving, after failing to meet the IRS's mandatory 5% payout requirement for several consecutive prior years. Grantmaking is managed by an unpaid board including Elon Musk, Jared Birchall, and Matilda Simon, with no full-time employees; a significant portion of grants flow through donor-advised funds at Vanguard and Fidelity, and to The Foundation, another Musk-affiliated entity. The foundation does not accept unsolicited proposals and maintains a famously minimal web presence. Tax filings indicate grantmaking closely aligns with Musk's personal business interests and objectives rather than a formal philanthropic strategy.
entities.belief_regulatory_stance_detail: The Musk Foundation's stated mission includes 'development of safe artificial intelligence to benefit humanity,' suggesting some concern for responsible AI development. However, Elon Musk's broader public stance on AI regulation has been inconsistent — he co-founded OpenAI and funded AI safety organizations, yet has also publicly opposed heavy-handed regulation and has launched his own AI company (xAI). Grants to organizations like CSET and Future of Life Institute suggest support for AI governance research, but overall posture leans away from restrictive regulation.

entities.name: NFDG Ventures
entities.notes: NFDG Ventures (also referred to simply as NFDG) is an investment partnership co-run by Nat Friedman (former CEO of GitHub) and Daniel Gross (CEO of Safe Superintelligence Inc.). The name 'NFDG' appears to be derived from the initials of Nat Friedman and Daniel Gross. NFDG served as the lead investor in SSI's landmark $1 billion Series A funding round announced September 4, 2024, which valued the three-month-old company at $5 billion. SSI was co-founded by former OpenAI chief scientist Ilya Sutskever, Daniel Gross, and former OpenAI researcher Daniel Levy, with a mission to develop safe superintelligence. Other investors in the round included Andreessen Horowitz, Sequoia Capital, DST Global, and SV Angel. NFDG's investment is notable given that Daniel Gross is simultaneously SSI's CEO, making NFDG a uniquely insider-positioned investor. The firm shapes the AI ecosystem through direct capital allocation to frontier AI safety research.

entities.name: RTX Ventures
entities.notes: RTX Ventures is the venture capital arm of RTX (formerly Raytheon Technologies, NYSE: RTX), one of the largest aerospace and defense contractors in the world. Led by President and Managing Director Dan (Daniel) Ateya, RTX Ventures invests in early-stage companies focused on transforming aerospace and defense, with portfolio areas including hypersonics, artificial intelligence, machine learning, advanced manufacturing, sustainable aviation, and space. The fund made its first eight investments in 2022, backing companies such as H55 (electric propulsion, October 2022) and Hermeus (hypersonics). In July 2023, RTX Ventures led a $45 million Series A round for Impulse Space, an in-space transportation company. In June 2025, RTX Ventures co-led a $5.5 million seed round for Phoenix Semiconductor, focused on domestically manufactured legacy chips for national security applications. In July 2025, RTX Ventures led a $55 million Series Seed+ round for Radical AI, a NYC-based company building autonomous labs for materials R&D using AI, alongside NVIDIA's NVentures, noa, Eni Next, Infinite Capital, and AlleyCorp. RTX Ventures shapes the AI ecosystem primarily through its role as a funder of AI-driven defense, materials science, and aerospace startups, leveraging RTX's engineering resources to accelerate portfolio company growth.
